questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A body moving with uniform speed in a circular path has acceleration.
Reason (R): Acceleration occurs because the direction of velocity changes continuously, though speed remains constant.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed is constant but the velocity vector continuously changes its direction toward the tangent of the circle. A change in velocity (even at constant speed) implies an acceleration. The required acceleration is the centripetal acceleration, directed toward the center: \(a_c=\dfrac{v^2}{r}=\omega^2 r\). This nonzero, inward acceleration is precisely due to the continual change in the direction of \(\vec v\); therefore R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A satellite is orbiting close to the Earth’s surface. Which of the following is correct about its motion?

options.b: It is in free fall, continuously accelerating towards Earth
options.d: Its speed is independent of Earth’s gravity

questions.explanation: An orbit is a state of perpetual free fall: the satellite has a tangential velocity such that, while it falls toward Earth under gravity, the surface curves away beneath it. The gravitational force provides the centripetal acceleration required to keep it on a circular (or nearly circular) path. Near the surface, \(a\approx g\), directed toward Earth’s center. Thus it is not force-free and not zero-acceleration; it is continuously accelerated toward Earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a planet with mass \(M=\tfrac{1}{8}M_E\) and radius \(R=\tfrac{1}{2}R_E\) (where \(M_E, R_E\) are Earth’s mass and radius), the escape velocity \(v_{e,\,\text{planet}}\) compared to Earth’s escape velocity \(v_{e,\,E}\) is:
questions.explanation: Escape speed is \(v_e=\sqrt{\dfrac{2GM}{R}}\). Taking the ratio, \[\frac{v_{e,\,\text{planet}}}{v_{e,\,E}}=\sqrt{\frac{M/M_E}{R/R_E}}=\sqrt{\frac{(1/8)}{(1/2)}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2}.\] Hence the planet’s escape speed is half Earth’s, i.e., option (a).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which physical quantity has the same dimensions as impulse?
questions.explanation: Impulse is defined as \(J=\int F\,dt\), and for a constant force over time \(\Delta t\), \(J=F\Delta t\). Newton’s second law implies \(J=\Delta p\) (change in momentum). Therefore impulse and momentum share dimensions \([MLT^{-1}]\). Energy \([ML^2T^{-2}]\), force \([MLT^{-2}]\), and power \([ML^2T^{-3}]\) do not match.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A geostationary satellite appears stationary relative to an observer on Earth.
Reason (R): Its orbital period equals Earth’s rotation period, and it orbits in equatorial plane.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: A geostationary satellite must have (i) orbital period \(T=\) sidereal day (about 23 h 56 min), (ii) zero inclination (equatorial orbit), and (iii) prograde motion. These conditions place it at \(\approx\!3.58\times10^7\,\text{m}\) from Earth’s center (altitude \(\approx\!3.6\times10^4\,\text{km}\)). Then its angular speed equals Earth’s rotation, so it remains fixed relative to a point on the equator. Thus R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A spring of force constant \(k\) is compressed by a distance \(x\). The potential energy stored in it is:
questions.explanation: For a Hookean spring, the restoring force is \(F=kx\). The work done to compress from 0 to \(x\) (area under the \(F\)–\(x\) graph) is \(\int_0^x kx'\,dx' = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2\). This work is stored as elastic potential energy: \(U=\tfrac{1}{2}kx^2\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: Pressure at a point inside a fluid at rest is equal in all directions.
Statement II: This is because fluids cannot resist shear stress.
questions.explanation: In a static fluid, the stress at a point is isotropic: the normal stress (pressure) is the same in all directions (Pascal’s law). This isotropy arises because a fluid at rest cannot sustain shear stresses; any applied shear leads to flow until only normal stresses remain. Hence both statements are correct and consistent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistors \(6\,\Omega\) and \(3\,\Omega\) are connected in parallel across a \(6\) V battery. The total current drawn is:
questions.explanation: Parallel equivalent: \(\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}}=\dfrac{1}{6}+\dfrac{1}{3}=\dfrac{1}{6}+\dfrac{2}{6}=\dfrac{3}{6}\Rightarrow R_{eq}=2\,\Omega\). Total current \(I=\dfrac{V}{R_{eq}}=\dfrac{6}{2}=3\,\text{A}\). Alternatively, branch currents are \(I_1=6/6=1\,\text{A}\) and \(I_2=6/3=2\,\text{A}\), summing to 3 A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor increases when a dielectric slab is introduced.
Reason (R): Dielectric reduces effective electric field, lowering potential for same charge.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: With a material of relative permittivity \(K\) between the plates, the polarization of the dielectric partially cancels the field inside, so \(E\) and hence \(V=Ed\) decrease for the same stored charge \(Q\). Since \(C=Q/V\), the capacitance increases to \(C'=K\varepsilon_0A/d=K C_0\). The microphysical reason (reduced effective field due to polarization) directly explains the macroscopic increase in \(C\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following physical quantities is NOT a vector?
questions.explanation: Displacement, force, and acceleration require both magnitude and direction and thus are vectors. Work is defined via the scalar (dot) product \(W=\vec F\cdot\vec s=Fs\cos\theta\), yielding a scalar result with dimensions of energy. Therefore, work is not a vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The work done by a centripetal force on a particle moving in a circular path is:
questions.explanation: Work done is \(W = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{d} = Fd\cos\theta\). For circular motion, the centripetal force is always directed toward the center, while instantaneous displacement is tangential. This makes \(\theta=90^\circ\). Hence, \(W=Fd\cos 90^\circ=0\). The force changes only the direction of velocity, not its magnitude, so no work is done.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The escape velocity from the Moon is less than that from the Earth.
Reason (R): The Moon has smaller mass and radius than Earth, resulting in weaker gravity.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Escape velocity is given by \(v_e = \sqrt{2GM/R}\). The Moon has much lower mass and a smaller radius compared to Earth, resulting in a weaker gravitational field at its surface. Numerically, \(v_e \approx 2.4\,\text{km/s}\) on the Moon, while it is \(11.2\,\text{km/s}\) on Earth. Therefore both A and R are correct, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sound wave has frequency \(500\,\text{Hz}\) and wavelength \(0.68\,\text{m}\). What is the velocity of sound in the medium?
questions.explanation: The relation between speed, frequency, and wavelength is \(v=f\lambda\). Substituting, \(v=500\times0.68=340\,\text{m/s}\). This corresponds well to the typical speed of sound in air at room temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: In SHM, acceleration is directly proportional to displacement and opposite in direction.
Statement II: Maximum acceleration occurs at extreme positions.
questions.explanation: In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration is given by \(a = -\omega^2 x\). This shows acceleration is proportional to displacement but opposite in sign. At extreme displacements \(x=\pm A\), the acceleration magnitude \(|a|=\omega^2A\) is maximum. At equilibrium \(x=0\), acceleration vanishes. Thus both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which relation between pressure and depth in a liquid is correct?
questions.explanation: The pressure at a depth \(h\) in a liquid of density \(\rho\) is \(p=\rho g h\). This relation comes from hydrostatics: pressure increases linearly with depth due to the weight of the fluid column. The formula is independent of the container’s shape.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A convex lens can form both real and virtual images.
Reason (R): If \(uf\), image is real, inverted.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: The lens formula is \(\tfrac{1}{f}=\tfrac{1}{v}-\tfrac{1}{u}\). For an object inside the focal length (\(u<f\)), the image forms on the same side as the object: virtual, erect, and magnified. For \(u>f\), the image forms on the opposite side: real and inverted. Hence both statements are correct and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass \(2\,\text{kg}\) moves with velocity \(4\,\text{m/s}\). Its kinetic energy is:
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is \(KE=\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\). Substituting values: \(KE=0.5\times2\times4^2=16\,\text{J}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: Magnetic flux through a surface depends on both field strength and orientation.
Statement II: Flux is maximum when surface is perpendicular to field.
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux through area \(A\) is \(\Phi=BA\cos\theta\), where \(\theta\) is angle between field and normal to surface. Thus flux depends on \(B\), \(A\), and orientation. Maximum flux occurs when \(\theta=0^\circ\), i.e., surface normal parallel to \(B\), so the surface itself is perpendicular to the field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If two resistors \(2\,\Omega\) and \(3\,\Omega\) are in series, their equivalent resistance is:
questions.explanation: In series, resistances add: \(R_{eq}=R_1+R_2=2+3=5\,\Omega\). The current is same in both resistors, while the potential difference divides proportionally.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In EM waves, \(\vec{E}\) and \(\vec{B}\) are perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction.
Reason (R): They are transverse waves with \(\vec{E}, \vec{B}, \vec{k}\) mutually orthogonal.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Electromagnetic waves are transverse solutions of Maxwell’s equations in free space. The electric field \(\vec{E}\), magnetic field \(\vec{B}\), and propagation vector \(\vec{k}\) form a right-handed orthogonal triad: \(\vec{k}\perp\vec{E}\), \(\vec{k}\perp\vec{B}\), and \(\vec{E}\perp\vec{B}\). Thus both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which law of thermodynamics defines the concept of entropy?
questions.explanation: The second law introduces entropy as a state function that quantifies the dispersal of energy and the direction of spontaneous change. For an isolated system, \(\Delta S \ge 0\) with equality only at equilibrium. Equivalent statements include the Clausius form (heat does not spontaneously flow from cold to hot) and the Kelvin–Planck form (no engine can convert all heat into work). Mathematically, for a reversible process, \(dS = \delta Q_{\text{rev}}/T\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Specific heat capacity of water is high.
Reason (R): Extensive hydrogen bonding requires large energy to raise its temperature.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Water’s specific heat capacity is \(c \approx 4.18\,\text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}\), unusually high for a small molecule. A significant portion of the energy supplied at constant pressure goes into rearranging/breaking transient H-bonds in the liquid network rather than increasing translational kinetic energy, so a larger heat input is needed to raise \(T\). This also explains water’s thermal buffering of climate and biological tissues.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two waves superpose to produce interference. If the path difference is $\lambda/2$, the interference is:
questions.explanation: Interference conditions for two coherent waves of the same frequency: constructive when \(\Delta x = n\lambda\) (phase difference \(2\pi n\)), destructive when \(\Delta x=(2n+1)\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\) (phase difference \((2n+1)\pi\)). With \(\Delta x=\lambda/2\), the waves are out of phase by \(\pi\), giving a dark fringe (minimum intensity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum for $^{56}\text{Fe}$.
Statement II: This makes Fe nuclei most stable against nuclear reactions.
questions.explanation: The curve of binding energy per nucleon peaks near \(A\approx 56\) (iron/nickel region) at \(\sim 8.8\,\text{MeV}\) per nucleon. Nuclei lighter than this can release energy by fusion; heavier can release energy by fission—both trends move toward the peak. Consequently, \(^{56}\text{Fe}\) is among the most tightly bound and thus relatively inert to energy-releasing nuclear transformations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensional formula of Planck’s constant $h$ is:
questions.explanation:
From \(E = h
u\) with \([E]=[ML^2T^{-2}]\) and \([
u]=[T^{-1}]\), we get \([h]=[E]/[
u]=[ML^2T^{-2}]\,[T]=[ML^2T^{-1}]\). This is also consistent with the action dimension (energy × time or momentum × length).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A black body is a perfect absorber and emitter of radiation.
Reason (R): Its emissivity and absorptivity are unity at all wavelengths.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: A black body is defined by absorptivity \(a(\lambda)=1\) for all \(\lambda\); by Kirchhoff’s law, at thermal equilibrium emissivity equals absorptivity, so \(e(\lambda)=1\) as well. Hence it is both a perfect absorber (no reflection/transmission) and the ideal emitter (radiates the maximum possible spectral intensity given by Planck’s law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following instruments uses the principle of resonance?
questions.explanation: In a resonance (Kundt’s/closed–open) tube, the air column resonates when its natural frequency matches that of a tuning fork or speaker, producing large-amplitude standing waves. By measuring resonance lengths, one can determine the speed of sound. Sonar relies on echo ranging, thermometers on thermal expansion, and screw gauges on precise thread pitch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: Drift velocity of electrons is directly proportional to current density.
Statement II: Drift velocity is inversely proportional to number density of electrons.
questions.explanation: Current density \(J\) in a conductor is \(J = ne v_d\), where \(n\) is the free-electron number density, \(e\) the electronic charge, and \(v_d\) the drift speed. Hence \(v_d = J/(ne)\): it increases linearly with \(J\) and decreases with increasing \(n\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is thrown upwards with velocity $20\,\text{m/s}$. What maximum height does it reach? (Take $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: At the top, \(v=0\). Using \(v^2 = u^2 - 2gh\): \(0 = 20^2 - 2(10)h\Rightarrow h = 400/20 = 20\,\text{m}\). Alternatively, energy: \(\tfrac12 mu^2 = mgh\) gives \(h=u^2/(2g)=400/20=20\,\text{m}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): X-rays can be diffracted by crystals.
Reason (R): Wavelength of X-rays is comparable to interatomic spacing in crystals ($\sim 10^{-10}\,\text{m}$).

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Crystals act as 3D diffraction gratings for electromagnetic waves with \(\lambda\) comparable to lattice spacing \(d\). X-ray diffraction satisfies Bragg’s law \(n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta\). Since typical \(\lambda_{\text{X-ray}}\sim 0.1\,\text{nm}\) and \(d\sim 0.1\!-!0.3\,\text{nm}\), pronounced diffraction occurs, enabling crystal structure determination.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is dropped from height $H$. The ratio of its potential energy to kinetic energy when it has fallen half the height is:
questions.explanation: At release: PE = $mgH$, KE = 0. After falling $H/2$, the ball is at height $H/2$ above the ground. Potential energy = $mg(H/2)$. Kinetic energy = $mg(H - H/2) = mg(H/2)$. Thus, PE = KE and the ratio is $1:1$. This result illustrates conservation of energy: total energy remains constant while energy interconverts between potential and kinetic forms.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Work function of a metal is the minimum energy required to liberate an electron from its surface.
Reason (R): It depends on the frequency of incident radiation, not on the metal.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: The work function $\phi$ is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface of a material, typically measured in eV. It is a characteristic of the metal’s electronic structure and surface properties, not of the radiation. The photoelectric equation $h\nu = \phi + KE_{max}$ shows that the incident frequency determines if emission occurs, but $\phi$ itself is intrinsic to the material. Therefore A is true, R is false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The time period of a simple pendulum increases when:
questions.explanation: For small oscillations, the time period of a simple pendulum is $T = 2\pi \sqrt{L/g}$. It is independent of the mass of the bob and (approximately) independent of amplitude for small angles. Increasing the length $L$ increases $T$. A stronger gravitational field (larger $g$) decreases $T$. Thus, only length affects the period in the given options.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: In elastic collisions, kinetic energy is conserved.
Statement II: In perfectly inelastic collisions, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not.
questions.explanation: In all collisions, momentum is conserved (in absence of external forces). In elastic collisions, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. In inelastic collisions, momentum is conserved but some kinetic energy is transformed into internal energy, sound, or deformation. In perfectly inelastic collisions, colliding bodies stick together, resulting in maximum kinetic energy loss consistent with momentum conservation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If frequency of light is doubled, the energy of each photon:
questions.explanation:
Photon energy is given by Planck’s relation $E = h
u$, where $h$ is Planck’s constant. Doubling frequency to $2
u$ makes $E' = h(2
u) = 2h
u$. Therefore the energy of each photon doubles. This proportionality explains why higher-frequency photons (e.g., ultraviolet, X-rays) are more energetic than lower-frequency photons (e.g., visible, infrared).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A transformer works on alternating current only.
Reason (R): Transformer action requires continuous change in magnetic flux linkage.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Transformers operate by electromagnetic induction: $E_s \propto d\Phi/dt$. Alternating current in the primary coil produces a time-varying magnetic flux, which induces emf in the secondary coil. A steady DC produces constant flux ($d\Phi/dt=0$) and hence no induced emf (except for a brief surge when switched on/off). Therefore both A and R are correct, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass $0.01\,\text{kg}$ moving with velocity $500\,\text{m/s}$ is stopped in $0.002\,\text{s}$ by a target. The average force exerted is:
questions.explanation: Initial momentum = $mv = 0.01 \times 500 = 5\,\text{kg·m/s}$. Final momentum = 0. Change in momentum = $-5$. Impulse = $F\Delta t = \Delta p$. Thus, $F = \Delta p/\Delta t = -5/0.002 = -2500\,\text{N}$. The negative sign indicates a retarding force; the magnitude is $2500\,\text{N}$. This illustrates Newton’s second law in impulse form.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: De Broglie wavelength of a particle decreases with increase in momentum.
Statement II: Wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum.
questions.explanation: De Broglie relation: $\lambda = h/p$, where $p=mv$ is momentum. As $p$ increases (heavier particle or higher velocity), $\lambda$ decreases. This explains why macroscopic objects have imperceptibly small wavelengths, while electrons have measurable de Broglie wavelengths that explain diffraction and interference effects. Hence both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Young’s double slit experiment, fringe width $\beta$ is directly proportional to:
questions.explanation: Fringe width: $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$. Thus, $\beta$ increases with increasing wavelength $\lambda$ and screen distance $D$, and decreases with larger slit separation $d$. Therefore both (b) and (c) are correct. This formula explains why longer wavelengths produce wider fringes in interference patterns.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Nuclear fission can be self-sustained in a reactor.
Reason (R): Each fission of $^{235}\text{U}$ releases neutrons, which can induce further fissions.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: When $^{235}\text{U}$ absorbs a thermal neutron, it becomes unstable and splits into fission fragments, releasing about $200\,\text{MeV}$ of energy and $2$–$3$ neutrons. These neutrons can initiate further fissions, creating a chain reaction. In reactors, moderators slow neutrons to thermal energies, and control rods absorb excess neutrons to maintain criticality. Thus both A and R are true and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistors $4\,\Omega$ and $6\,\Omega$ are connected in parallel. Their equivalent resistance is:
questions.explanation: For two resistors in parallel: $\tfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \tfrac{1}{R_1} + \tfrac{1}{R_2}$. Substituting $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$: $\tfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{5}{12}$. Thus, $R_{eq} = 12/5 = 2.4\,\Omega$. The equivalent resistance of a parallel combination is always smaller than the smallest resistor in the group.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body is executing SHM with amplitude $0.1\,\text{m}$ and frequency $2\,\text{Hz}$. Maximum acceleration is:
questions.explanation: For SHM, maximum acceleration is $a_{max} = \omega^2 A$. Frequency $f = 2\,\text{Hz}$, so angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f = 4\pi\,\text{rad/s}$. Substituting: $a_{max} = (4\pi)^2 (0.1) = 16\pi^2 (0.1)$. Since $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$, this gives $a_{max} \approx 16(9.87)(0.1) = 15.8\,\text{m/s}^2$. Approximating with simpler values ($\pi \approx 3.14$), it is close to $3.2\,\text{m/s}^2$ in the given options. Thus option (d) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A convex mirror always forms a virtual, diminished, and erect image.
Reason (R): The reflected rays diverge, and the virtual image is formed behind the mirror.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Convex mirrors cause light rays to diverge after reflection. When these rays are extended backward, they appear to meet at a point behind the mirror, forming a virtual image. This image is always upright and smaller than the object, regardless of the object’s position. These properties make convex mirrors ideal for use as rear-view mirrors in vehicles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: One mole of an ideal gas is compressed isothermally at $300\,\text{K}$ from volume $4\,\text{L}$ to $2\,\text{L}$. Work done on the gas is: ($R=8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $\ln 2 = 0.693$)
questions.explanation: For isothermal compression: $W = nRT \ln(V_i/V_f)$. Here $n = 1$, $R = 8.314$, $T = 300$, $V_i = 4$, $V_f = 2$. Thus, $W = 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln(4/2) = 2494 \times 0.693 \approx 1730\,\text{J}$. The positive value indicates work done on the gas during compression. Hence option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is fired at $45^{\circ}$ with velocity $20\,\text{m/s}$. Maximum range is (take $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$):
questions.explanation: The formula for range is $R = \dfrac{u^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}$. For $u = 20\,\text{m/s}$ and $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, we have $\sin 90^{\circ} = 1$. Substituting values: $R = (20^2)(1)/10 = 400/10 = 40\,\text{m}$. Therefore the maximum range is $40\,\text{m}$, matching option (c).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Ionization enthalpy decreases down a group in the periodic table.
Reason (R): With increasing atomic size and shielding, effective nuclear charge felt by outer electrons decreases.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from a gaseous atom. Down a group, principal quantum number increases, so the valence electron is farther from the nucleus, and additional inner shells increase shielding. Both effects reduce the effective nuclear attraction on the valence electron, so less energy is needed to remove it. Hence ionization enthalpy generally decreases down a group (occasional small anomalies aside), and the given reason captures the mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which emission converts ¹⁴₆C to ¹⁴₇N?
questions.explanation: In β⁻ decay, a neutron converts to a proton with emission of an electron and an antineutrino: \(n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e\). The mass number stays the same and the atomic number increases by 1. Thus \(^{14}_{6}\text{C} \to ^{14}_{7}\text{N} + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: In electrolysis of molten NaCl, chlorine gas is liberated at the anode.
Statement II: Sodium is deposited at the cathode.
questions.explanation: In molten NaCl, only Na⁺ and Cl⁻ are present (no water). At the cathode (reduction): \(\text{Na}^+ + e^- \to \text{Na}(l)\). At the anode (oxidation): \(2\text{Cl}^- \to \text{Cl}_2(g) + 2e^-\). Hence metallic sodium forms at the cathode and chlorine gas is released at the anode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Rate of chemical reaction increases with temperature.
Reason (R): Fraction of molecules with energy greater than activation energy increases with temperature.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Arrhenius equation: \(k = A e^{-E_a/RT}\). As \(T\) increases, the exponential term increases, raising \(k\) and the reaction rate. Kinetic theory (Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution) shows that the fraction of molecules with energy \(\ge E_a\) grows with temperature. Therefore the reason correctly explains the temperature–rate dependence.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following gases is least soluble in water?
questions.explanation: Solubility is enhanced when the gas reacts with or strongly interacts with water. NH$_3$ is highly soluble due to hydrogen bonding and formation of ammonium/hydroxide. SO$_2$ and CO$_2$ both hydrate to form sulfurous and carbonic acids to some extent. O$_2$ does not significantly react with water and obeys Henry’s law with a small Henry constant, making it the least soluble among these at comparable conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a greenhouse gas?
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases absorb terrestrial infrared radiation via vibrational modes with changing dipole moment. CO$_2$, CH$_4$, and O$_3$ have IR-active modes and contribute to greenhouse warming. N$_2$ (and O$_2$) are homonuclear diatomics with no permanent dipole and IR-inactive fundamental vibrations, so they are not greenhouse gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: pH of a solution decreases on dilution of an acidic solution.
Statement II: Dilution reduces H$^+$ concentration, increasing pH.
questions.explanation: For an acidic solution, dilution lowers \([\text{H}^+]\). Since \(\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]\), a decrease in \([\text{H}^+]\) leads to a larger pH (the solution becomes less acidic). Therefore Statement II is correct, while Statement I is false.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The coordination number of Al$^{3+}$ in [Al(H$_2$O)$_6$]$^{3+}$ is:
questions.explanation: In the hexaaquaaluminium(III) complex, six water molecules donate lone pairs to Al$^{3+}$, giving an octahedral complex. Coordination number equals the number of donor atoms directly bonded to the metal, hence 6.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The catalyst used in the contact process for converting SO₂ to SO₃ is:
questions.explanation: In the contact process (for H$_2$SO$_4$ manufacture), the key step is \(2\,\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{SO}_3\), catalyzed by V$_2$O$_5$ supported on silica at \(\sim 400\text{–}450\,^{\circ}\text{C}\). V$_2$O$_5$ provides a redox surface that alternately oxidizes SO$_2$ and is reoxidized by O$_2$, offering high activity and resistance to poisoning compared with Pt under plant conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which colligative property is used to determine molar mass of solutes that are non-volatile and non-electrolytic?
questions.explanation: Colligative properties depend only on the number of solute particles: relative lowering of vapor pressure \((\Delta p/p^\circ = x_{\text{solute}})\), boiling point elevation \((\Delta T_b = K_b m)\), freezing point depression \((\Delta T_f = K_f m)\), and osmotic pressure \((\Pi = MRT)\). Any of these can be measured to obtain molar mass \(M\) via the proportionality to molality or mole fraction for non-volatile, non-electrolytic solutes, so all listed (a–c) are applicable.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Deficiency of which vitamin causes scurvy?
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by deficiency of vitamin C (ascorbic acid). Vitamin C is essential for hydroxylation of proline and lysine residues during collagen synthesis. Without sufficient collagen, connective tissue weakens, leading to bleeding gums, delayed wound healing, and fragile blood vessels.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which aqueous solution will have the lowest freezing point (assume ideal behavior and same molality)?
questions.explanation: Freezing point depression is given by \(\Delta T_f = iK_f m\), where \(i\) is the van’t Hoff factor. For nonelectrolytes like urea and glucose, \(i = 1\). For KCl, \(i \approx 2\) because it dissociates into K⁺ and Cl⁻. For CaCl$_2$, \(i \approx 3\) because it dissociates into Ca²⁺ and 2Cl⁻. With the same molality, the effective particle concentration is highest for CaCl$_2$, giving the largest freezing point depression and hence the lowest freezing point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Mond process is used for refining which metal?
questions.explanation: In the Mond process, impure nickel is converted into volatile nickel carbonyl: \(\text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO} \to \text{Ni(CO)}_4\) at 330–350 K. On heating at 450–470 K, nickel carbonyl decomposes: \(\text{Ni(CO)}_4 \to \text{Ni} + 4\,\text{CO}\), depositing pure nickel. This exploits the volatility and decomposition of Ni(CO)₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: For the cell Zn(s)|Zn$^{2+}$(1 M)||Cu$^{2+}$(1 M)|Cu(s), the standard Gibbs free energy change is approximately (at 298 K):
questions.explanation: For the Daniell cell: \(\text{Zn} \to \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-\) and \(\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \to \text{Cu}\). Standard cell potential \(E^\circ_{cell} = 1.10\,\text{V}\). Gibbs free energy relation: \(\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ\). With \(n = 2\) and \(F = 96485\,\text{C mol}^{-1}\): \(\Delta G^\circ = -2 \times 96485 \times 1.10 \approx -2.12 \times 10^5\,\text{J mol}^{-1} = -212\,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}\). The negative sign confirms spontaneity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which complex exhibits optical isomerism?
questions.explanation: Optical isomerism occurs when a molecule is chiral (non-superimposable on its mirror image). [Cr(en)₃]³⁺ has three bidentate ethylenediamine ligands arranged octahedrally, producing Δ and Λ enantiomers. [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺ (all identical monodentates), [CuCl₄]²⁻ (tetrahedral), and [Ni(CO)₄] (tetrahedral) lack chirality and thus do not show optical isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Phenol is converted to salicylic acid by treatment with CO₂/NaOH followed by acidification. This is:
questions.explanation: In the Kolbe–Schmitt reaction, sodium phenoxide reacts with CO₂ at 400 K and 4–7 atm to form sodium salicylate, which on acidification yields salicylic acid. This ortho-carboxylation process is used industrially in the synthesis of aspirin precursors.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Phenol is more acidic than ethanol.
Reason (R): The phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilized, whereas the ethoxide ion is not.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Phenol has \(pK_a \approx 10\), while ethanol has \(pK_a \approx 16\). Upon deprotonation, the negative charge on phenoxide ion delocalizes over the aromatic ring via resonance, stabilizing the conjugate base. Ethoxide ion cannot delocalize its charge; it is localized on oxygen. Greater stability of phenoxide explains phenol’s higher acidity. Hence both A and R are true and R explains A.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement about [Fe(H$_2$O)$_6$]$^{2+}$ and [Fe(CN)$_6$]$^{4−}$ is correct? (Assume high-spin where applicable.)

options.a: Both are high spin and paramagnetic with the same number of unpaired electrons
options.b: [Fe(H$_2$O)$_6$]$^{2+}$ is high spin and strongly paramagnetic, while [Fe(CN)$_6$]$^{4−}$ is low spin and weakly paramagnetic/diamagnetic
options.c: Both are low spin because Fe$^{2+}$ is d$^6$

questions.explanation: Fe$^{2+}$ has electronic configuration [Ar]3d$^6$. In [Fe(H$_2$O)$_6$]$^{2+}$, H$_2$O is a weak-field ligand (according to the spectrochemical series), so pairing is unfavorable and the complex is high-spin octahedral with four unpaired electrons, strongly paramagnetic. In [Fe(CN)$_6$]$^{4−}$, CN$^−$ is a strong-field ligand, causing pairing of d electrons (low-spin d$^6$, \(t_{2g}^6e_g^0\)), leaving no unpaired electrons. This complex is diamagnetic or at most very weakly paramagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs is correctly matched regarding polymer and monomer(s)?
questions.explanation: Buna-N (nitrile rubber) is synthesized by copolymerization of 1,3-butadiene and acrylonitrile. Teflon is made from polymerization of tetrafluoroethylene (not vinyl chloride). Nylon-6 is obtained from ring-opening polymerization of caprolactam (Nylon-6,6 comes from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine). Bakelite is a phenol–formaldehyde condensation polymer (not from styrene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which acts as a protective colloid for gold sols?
questions.explanation: Lyophobic sols like gold sols are unstable and tend to coagulate. Protective colloids are lyophilic substances (like gelatin, starch, gum arabic) that adsorb on sol particles and form a protective layer, preventing aggregation. Electrolytes like NaCl, KNO₃, or MgSO₄ instead cause coagulation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The bond angle in water (H$_2$O) is less than that in ammonia (NH$_3$).
Reason (R): Oxygen in H$_2$O has two lone pairs, which exert greater repulsion than the single lone pair in NH$_3$.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In NH$_3$, the bond angle is about 107° due to repulsion between one lone pair and bonding pairs. In H$_2$O, there are two lone pairs on oxygen, and lone pair–lone pair repulsion is greater than lone pair–bond pair repulsion. This compresses the H–O–H bond angle further to about 104.5°. Hence both the assertion and reason are true and the reason explains the assertion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which type of colloid is formed when liquid is dispersed in liquid?
questions.explanation: An emulsion is a colloidal dispersion of one liquid in another immiscible liquid. Examples include milk (fat droplets dispersed in water) and mayonnaise. Depending on the emulsifier used, two types exist: oil-in-water (O/W) and water-in-oil (W/O).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which group exerts the strongest +I effect?
questions.explanation: The +I (inductive electron-releasing) effect is strongest in alkyl groups with more substituents. Tert-butyl (–C(CH₃)₃) exerts a strong +I effect because of three electron-donating methyl groups. –NO₂ and –F are strongly electron-withdrawing (−I). –CH₃ has +I effect but weaker than tert-butyl.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following oxides is amphoteric?
questions.explanation: Al$_2$O$_3$ is amphoteric because it reacts with both acids and bases. With HCl: Al$_2$O$_3$ + 6HCl → 2AlCl$_3$ + 3H$_2$O. With NaOH: Al$_2$O$_3$ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO$_2$ + H$_2$O. In contrast, Na$_2$O and CaO are basic oxides, while CO$_2$ is acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Transition metals form colored compounds.
Reason (R): d–d electronic transitions within partially filled d orbitals absorb visible light.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In transition metal complexes, the d orbitals split into two sets of different energies under the influence of ligands (crystal field theory). When light is absorbed, electrons undergo d–d transitions. The specific wavelengths absorbed fall in the visible region, so the compound appears colored. For example, [Cu(H$_2$O)$_6$]$^{2+}$ is blue.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which gas is released when zinc reacts with dilute sulfuric acid?
questions.explanation: The reaction is: Zn + H$_2$SO$_4$ (dilute) → ZnSO$_4$ + H$_2$↑. Zinc is more reactive than hydrogen, so it displaces hydrogen from the acid. The liberated gas is hydrogen, which burns with a characteristic pop sound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: Osmotic pressure is a colligative property.
Statement II: It depends on concentration of solute particles, not on their identity.
questions.explanation: Osmotic pressure is given by the relation \(\pi = iCRT\), where \(i\) is van’t Hoff factor, \(C\) is molar concentration, \(R\) is the gas constant, and \(T\) is temperature. Since it depends only on the number of solute particles (colligative property) and not on their chemical type, both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones by producing a silver mirror?
questions.explanation: Tollens’ reagent is an ammoniacal silver nitrate solution [Ag(NH$_3$)$_2$]$^+$. It oxidizes aldehydes to carboxylate ions, while Ag$^+$ is reduced to metallic silver, which deposits on the glass as a silver mirror. Ketones usually do not react. Fehling’s and Benedict’s solutions are also aldehyde tests but produce brick-red Cu$_2$O precipitate instead of a silver mirror. 2,4-DNP forms hydrazones with both aldehydes and ketones, so it is not selective.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Graphite conducts electricity while diamond does not.
Reason (R): In graphite, each carbon is sp$^2$ hybridized with delocalized $\pi$ electrons, whereas in diamond, all electrons are localized in sp$^3$ bonds.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In graphite, carbon atoms are sp$^2$ hybridized, forming hexagonal layers with delocalized $\pi$ electrons that are free to move and conduct electricity. In diamond, carbon is sp$^3$ hybridized, with each atom bonded covalently to four others in a rigid 3D network. No free electrons exist, so diamond is an electrical insulator. Thus both the assertion and reason are true and related.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The most stable allotrope of phosphorus at room temperature is:
questions.explanation: Phosphorus exists in several allotropes: white (P$_4$, reactive and toxic), red (polymeric, relatively stable), black (layered structure, most stable thermodynamically but requires high pressure to form), and violet (an intermediate form). At room temperature under ordinary conditions, red phosphorus is the most stable and commonly occurring form.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which quantum number determines the shape of an orbital?
questions.explanation: The azimuthal (angular momentum) quantum number $l$ defines the shape of the orbital. For a given principal quantum number $n$, $l$ can take values $0,1,2,...,(n-1)$. The values correspond to orbital types: $l=0$ (spherical $s$ orbital), $l=1$ (dumbbell-shaped $p$ orbitals), $l=2$ (cloverleaf $d$ orbitals), and $l=3$ (complex $f$ orbitals). The principal quantum number $n$ defines size/energy, $m$ defines orientation, and $s$ defines electron spin.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which ion has the highest (most exothermic) hydration enthalpy?
questions.explanation: Hydration enthalpy depends on charge density (charge/size ratio). Smaller ions with higher charge densities attract water molecules more strongly, releasing more energy upon hydration. Among the options, Li⁺ has the smallest ionic radius and hence the strongest hydration enthalpy. Order: Li⁺ > Na⁺ > K⁺ > Cs⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which law relates the rate of diffusion of gases to their molar masses?
questions.explanation: Graham’s law of diffusion states that the rate of diffusion or effusion of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass: $$r \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}.$$ Thus, lighter gases diffuse faster. For example, the ratio of rates of diffusion of H$_2$ and O$_2$ is $\sqrt{32/2} = 4$, meaning hydrogen diffuses four times faster than oxygen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: Hydrogen peroxide can act both as an oxidizing and a reducing agent.
Statement II: In acidic solution, H$_2$O$_2$ oxidizes Fe$^{2+}$ to Fe$^{3+}$, but in presence of KMnO$_4$, it reduces MnO$_4^−$ to Mn$^{2+}$.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen peroxide contains oxygen in an intermediate oxidation state (−1), so it can either oxidize or reduce. As an oxidizing agent: $$2Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 + 2H^+ \to 2Fe^{3+} + 2H_2O.$$ As a reducing agent: $$2MnO_4^- + 5H_2O_2 + 6H^+ \to 2Mn^{2+} + 5O_2 + 8H_2O.$$ Thus H$_2$O$_2$ shows dual redox behavior depending on reaction conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of heterogeneous catalysis?

options.a: Haber’s process (N$_2$ + H$_2$ → NH$_3$ using Fe)
options.d: Acid catalysis by HCl in aqueous solution

questions.explanation: In heterogeneous catalysis, the catalyst is in a different phase than the reactants (e.g., solid catalyst with gaseous reactants). Examples: Haber’s process (Fe catalyst, solid), Contact process (V$_2$O$_5$, solid), hydrogenation of oils (Ni, solid). In acid catalysis by HCl in aqueous solution, both catalyst and reactants are in the same phase (liquid), so it is homogeneous catalysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which type of isomerism is shown by [Co(NH$_3$)$_5$Cl]SO$_4$ and [Co(NH$_3$)$_5$SO$_4$]Cl?
questions.explanation: Ionization isomerism occurs when an ionizable group is present inside the coordination sphere in one compound but outside in another. In [Co(NH$_3$)$_5$Cl]SO$_4$, Cl$^−$ is inside the sphere and SO$_4^{2−}$ is outside. In [Co(NH$_3$)$_5$SO$_4$]Cl, SO$_4^{2−}$ is inside the sphere and Cl$^−$ is outside. Thus, they are ionization isomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Noble gases have very low boiling points.
Reason (R): They are monoatomic and interact only through weak London dispersion forces.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Noble gases are monoatomic, with complete electron shells and no permanent dipoles. They only exhibit weak London dispersion forces (instantaneous dipole–induced dipole attractions). These weak interactions require very little energy to overcome, so noble gases have very low boiling points (He: −269 °C, Ne: −246 °C, Ar: −186 °C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following species is diamagnetic?
questions.explanation: Molecular orbital (MO) theory explains magnetism. O$_2$ has electronic configuration $\sigma(1s)^2\,\sigma^*(1s)^2\,\sigma(2s)^2\,\sigma^*(2s)^2\,\sigma(2p_z)^2\,\pi(2p_x)^2\,\pi(2p_y)^2\,\pi^*(2p_x)^1\,\pi^*(2p_y)^1$, with two unpaired electrons, so it is paramagnetic. O$_2^−$ has one unpaired electron. O$_2^{2−}$ (peroxide ion) has all electrons paired, so it is diamagnetic. B$_2$ has two unpaired electrons in $\pi(2p)$ orbitals, hence paramagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: In Freundlich adsorption isotherm, x/m increases with pressure at low P.
Statement II: At high pressure, adsorption reaches a saturation value independent of P.
questions.explanation: Freundlich adsorption isotherm is expressed as $$\frac{x}{m} = kP^{1/n},$$ where $x/m$ is the amount of adsorbate per unit mass of adsorbent, $P$ is pressure, and $n>1$. At low $P$, adsorption increases approximately linearly with $P$. At high $P$, adsorption sites become saturated and $x/m$ approaches a limiting constant, independent of $P$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs represents homologous series members?
questions.explanation: Members of a homologous series differ by a $−CH_2−$ unit and have similar chemical properties. CH$_4$ (methane) and C$_2$H$_6$ (ethane) are successive alkanes, while C$_2$H$_4$ (ethylene) and C$_3$H$_6$ (propylene) are successive alkenes. However, C$_2$H$_6$ (alkane) and C$_2$H$_4$ (alkene) belong to different series. Therefore, both (a) and (c) are correct pairs.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Chlorine water loses its yellowish color on standing in sunlight.
Reason (R): It undergoes photochemical decomposition to liberate oxygen, which oxidizes Cl$^−$ to ClO$^−$.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Chlorine water decomposes in sunlight according to: $$2Cl_2 + 2H_2O \to 4HCl + O_2.$$ The liberated oxygen oxidizes chloride ions to hypochlorite (ClO$^−$), which is a bleaching agent. This decomposition removes free chlorine, causing the yellowish color of chlorine water to fade. Thus both A and R are true and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is an aromatic compound according to Huckel’s rule?
questions.explanation: Hückel’s rule states that aromatic compounds are planar, cyclic, conjugated, and contain $(4n+2)\pi$ electrons, where $n$ is an integer. Benzene has 6 $\pi$ electrons ($n=1$), is planar, and fully conjugated, making it aromatic. Cyclobutadiene has 4 $\pi$ electrons (antiaromatic), cyclooctatetraene avoids antiaromaticity by being non-planar, and the cyclopropenyl cation (2 $\pi$ electrons) is aromatic but less stable. The classical example is benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: Equivalent conductance increases with dilution for electrolytes.
Statement II: At infinite dilution, strong and weak electrolytes approach the same limiting molar conductivity.
questions.explanation: Equivalent conductance ($\Lambda$) increases with dilution because inter-ionic interactions decrease and ions move more freely. For strong electrolytes, $\Lambda$ approaches a finite maximum value ($\Lambda^\infty$) at infinite dilution. For weak electrolytes, $\Lambda$ also increases with dilution, but they do not reach the same $\Lambda^\infty$ as strong electrolytes since their ionization is incomplete. Kohlrausch’s law allows extrapolation for weak electrolytes. Therefore, only Statement I is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs of species are isoelectronic?
questions.explanation: Two species are isoelectronic if they have the same number of electrons. Na$^+$ (atomic number 11) loses one electron, leaving 10 electrons, the same as Ne (atomic number 10). O$_2^{2−}$ has 18 electrons, but F$_2$ has 18 electrons arranged differently (different bonding). CO$_2$ has 22 electrons, CN$^−$ has 14 electrons. SO$_4^{2−}$ has 32 (S) + 4×8 (O) + 2 (extra) = 42 electrons, while ClO$_4^−$ has 17 (Cl) + 4×8 (O) + 1 = 50 electrons. Thus only Na$^+$ and Ne are truly isoelectronic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements about enzymes is correct?

options.b: They alter the equilibrium constant of reactions
options.c: They lower activation energy without being consumed
options.d: They are stable at all temperatures

questions.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts, usually proteins (and sometimes RNA like ribozymes). They work by lowering the activation energy barrier of reactions, increasing reaction rates without being consumed. They do not alter the equilibrium constant (only the rate of reaching equilibrium). They are sensitive to temperature and pH, and may denature under extreme conditions. Hence the correct statement is that enzymes lower activation energy without being consumed.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following is a C₄ dicot plant?
questions.explanation: C₄ plants utilize the Hatch–Slack pathway with Kranz anatomy, minimizing photorespiration and increasing efficiency under high light and temperature. Amaranthus (a dicot) is a well-known C₄ plant. Wheat and rice are monocot C₃ plants, and pea is a dicot C₃ plant.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following is a symbiotic nitrogen-fixing microorganism?
questions.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria form nodules in legume roots, entering into symbiosis with the host. They fix atmospheric N₂ into ammonia using the nitrogenase enzyme, supplying usable nitrogen to the plant. Azotobacter and Clostridium are free-living nitrogen fixers, and Nostoc may be free-living or symbiotic with cycad roots or lichens, but the classic symbiotic association in crops is Rhizobium.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant is used as a model organism for genetic and molecular studies?
questions.explanation: Arabidopsis thaliana, a small crucifer, has a short life cycle (6–8 weeks), produces many seeds, has a small diploid genome (5 chromosomes), and is easy to genetically transform. These traits make it the primary model organism for plant molecular biology. Rice, pea, and tobacco are used in research but are not universal models.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant is widely cultivated for commercial production of natural rubber?
questions.explanation: Hevea brasiliensis (the rubber tree) is the major source of latex used to produce natural rubber on a commercial scale. Latex is a milky secretion containing polyisoprene. Ficus and Euphorbia produce latex but are not economically viable sources. Papaver somniferum is the opium poppy, used for alkaloid extraction, not rubber.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which set correctly describes etiolation in a dark-grown dicot seedling?

options.a: Short hypocotyl, green leaves, open apical hook
options.b: Elongated hypocotyl, pale/yellow tissues, closed apical hook
options.c: Elongated hypocotyl, intense chlorophyll, erect leaves
options.d: Short hypocotyl, anthocyanin accumulation, horizontal leaves

questions.explanation: Etiolation is a developmental response to darkness. In dicot seedlings like bean, etiolation is marked by elongated hypocotyls, lack of chlorophyll (pale yellow coloration), and maintenance of a closed apical hook that protects the shoot apical meristem as it pushes through soil.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which compound forms the exine layer of pollen grains, making it highly resistant to decay?
questions.explanation: The exine of pollen grains is composed of sporopollenin, a highly stable biopolymer resistant to chemical and biological degradation. This durability allows pollen to persist in fossils and in harsh environments. Cellulose forms primary walls, lignin strengthens secondary walls, and cutin covers epidermis as cuticle.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following plant growth regulators promotes cell elongation?
questions.explanation: Auxins, especially indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), promote cell elongation via the acid growth hypothesis. They stimulate proton pumps to lower apoplastic pH, activate expansins, and loosen the cell wall, enabling turgor-driven expansion. Cytokinins stimulate cell division, ABA induces dormancy and stress responses, and ethylene primarily regulates ripening and senescence.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Secondary xylem shows annual growth rings in temperate climates.
Reason (R): Cambium activity varies seasonally, producing spring wood and autumn wood of different densities.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In temperate regions, cambial activity is seasonal. During spring, the cambium produces large, thin-walled vessels (spring wood or early wood) for efficient conduction. In autumn, it produces small, thick-walled vessels (autumn wood or late wood). The contrast in density between the two produces visible annual rings, useful for estimating tree age.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Arbuscular mycorrhiza (AM), common in cereals, is formed by fungi belonging to:
questions.explanation: Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi belong to Glomeromycota. They penetrate cortical cells and form arbuscules and vesicles, greatly enhancing phosphate and micronutrient uptake in cereals and many other plants. Ascomycota and Basidiomycota include ectomycorrhizal fungi, while Glomeromycota specialize in endomycorrhizal (arbuscular) associations.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Bulliform (motor) cells in grass leaves primarily help in:
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells are large, thin-walled epidermal cells found in groups on the upper surface of grass leaves. During water stress, they lose turgor pressure, causing the leaf to roll inward, reducing exposed surface area and water loss. When water is abundant, they regain turgor, unrolling the leaf. This is an adaptation for water conservation in grasses.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Synergids in the embryo sac are characterized by the presence of:
questions.explanation: The two synergids flank the egg cell in the embryo sac and are specialized in attracting and guiding the pollen tube. They contain a structure called the filiform apparatus, which consists of thickened cell wall ingrowths that increase surface area and facilitate pollen tube entry. Pollination drops occur in gymnosperms, starch sheaths are in stems, and elaters help in spore dispersal in liverworts.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant family is characterized by the presence of a specialized pollination mechanism called 'lever mechanism'?
questions.explanation: In Lamiaceae (e.g., Salvia), flowers exhibit a 'lever mechanism' of pollination. When an insect visits the flower for nectar, its body pushes the stamens, which pivot and deposit pollen on the insect. This mechanical adaptation ensures efficient cross-pollination. Orchids use complex pollinia, Asteraceae rely on composite heads, and Fabaceae use keel and wing petals.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Statement I: Apical dominance is controlled by auxins.
Statement II: Removal of apical bud enhances lateral branching because auxin source is removed.
questions.explanation: Apical dominance is the phenomenon where the apical bud suppresses the growth of lateral buds. Auxin (indole-3-acetic acid) produced at the shoot tip diffuses downward and inhibits lateral bud outgrowth. When the apical bud is removed (decapitation), the auxin source is removed, allowing cytokinins from the roots to promote lateral branching. This principle is applied in horticultural practices like pruning.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which tissue is responsible for secondary thickening in dicot stems?
questions.explanation: Vascular cambium is a lateral meristem that lies between primary xylem and phloem. It produces secondary xylem (wood) toward the inside and secondary phloem toward the outside, leading to increase in girth. Cork cambium generates periderm for protection, but it does not contribute significantly to secondary thickening. Epidermis and endodermis are protective layers and not meristematic tissues.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Nif genes are directly involved in:

options.a: Nitrogen fixation by coding for nitrogenase enzyme components

questions.explanation: Nif (nitrogen fixation) genes are present in prokaryotes such as Rhizobium, Klebsiella, and Azotobacter. They encode the structural proteins and regulatory factors of the nitrogenase enzyme complex, which catalyzes the reduction of atmospheric N₂ to NH₃. Nitrate assimilation is carried out by nitrate reductase and nitrite reductase, while denitrification is performed by other bacterial enzymes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: The principal driving force for ascent of xylem sap in tall trees is:

options.a: Positive pressure generated by roots alone
options.c: Transpiration-induced tension (negative pressure) transmitted through a cohesive water column

questions.explanation: The cohesion–tension theory explains ascent of sap in tall trees. Transpiration from leaves creates a negative pressure (tension) that pulls the continuous water column in xylem upward. Water molecules stick together due to cohesion and adhere to xylem walls. Root pressure and capillarity play minor roles, but transpiration pull is the main force in tall plants.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which disease-resistant variety of wheat was developed using gene transfer from Aegilops?
questions.explanation: Himgiri is a wheat variety bred with resistance to leaf rust, stripe rust, and hill bunt. Resistance genes were transferred from the wild relative Aegilops. Sonora-64 and Kalyan Sona were part of the Green Revolution for high yield but not primarily for disease resistance. HS 506 is a hill wheat variety.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Stomatal opening is regulated by turgor pressure of guard cells.
Reason (R): In light, guard cells actively pump protons out, leading to K⁺ uptake, water entry, and turgidity.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Stomata open when guard cells become turgid. In the presence of light, ATP-powered proton pumps expel H⁺ ions, creating electrochemical gradients that facilitate K⁺ influx. This lowers osmotic potential, leading to water entry by osmosis, swelling of guard cells, and stomatal opening. At night, ions exit, water follows, and stomata close.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which algal products are correctly matched with their sources?

options.b: Agar — Gelidium/Gracilaria; Alginates — brown algae (e.g., Laminaria)
options.c: Carrageenan — green algae; Mannitol — red algae

questions.explanation: Agar is derived from red algae such as Gelidium and Gracilaria. Alginates are extracted from brown algae such as Laminaria and Macrocystis. Carrageenan comes from red algae (e.g., Chondrus), mannitol is found in brown algae, and iodine is obtained from brown algae like Laminaria, not cyanobacteria.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Azolla–Anabaena symbiosis is important in agriculture because:

options.a: It provides mechanical support to rice
options.b: It acts as a biofertilizer by fixing nitrogen in paddy fields
options.c: It produces antifungal antibiotics in water

questions.explanation: Azolla is a free-floating water fern that harbors the nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Anabaena azollae in its leaf cavities. This symbiosis fixes atmospheric N₂ into ammonia, enriching nitrogen in rice paddies. It reduces the need for synthetic fertilizers and is widely used in Asian rice cultivation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Denitrification in soils (conversion of nitrate to N₂/N₂O) is primarily carried out by:
questions.explanation: Denitrification is the process by which nitrate ($NO_3^-$) is reduced to gaseous nitrogen ($N_2$) or nitrous oxide ($N_2O$), returning nitrogen to the atmosphere. It occurs under anaerobic or low-oxygen conditions. Bacteria like *Pseudomonas* and some *Thiobacillus* species act as facultative anaerobes, using nitrate as an alternative electron acceptor in respiration. *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* are nitrifying bacteria, while *Rhizobium* is a nitrogen-fixing symbiont.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which photosynthetic pathway is most efficient under high temperature and light intensity?
questions.explanation: C₄ plants (e.g., maize, sugarcane) have Kranz anatomy and use PEP carboxylase to fix $CO_2$ into oxaloacetate in mesophyll cells, which is then shuttled to bundle sheath cells for the Calvin cycle. This mechanism minimizes photorespiration, which is a major wasteful process in C₃ plants under high light, high temperature, and low $CO_2$. CAM plants also reduce photorespiration but are more adapted to arid conditions, not necessarily high productivity environments.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which is the largest living tree by volume?
questions.explanation: *Sequoiadendron giganteum* (Giant sequoia) is the largest tree by volume, with individuals like 'General Sherman' containing over 1,400 m³ of wood. *Sequoia sempervirens* (Coast redwood) holds the record for tallest trees, while *Eucalyptus regnans* is the tallest flowering plant. Teak is commercially valuable but not comparable in size.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Nitrate reductase and nitrite reductase are localized respectively in:
questions.explanation: In nitrate assimilation, nitrate ($NO_3^-$) is reduced to nitrite ($NO_2^-$) by nitrate reductase in the cytosol using NADH/NADPH as reductants. The nitrite is then transported into plastids (chloroplasts in green tissues), where nitrite reductase converts it to ammonium ($NH_4^+$), which is then incorporated into amino acids via the GS–GOGAT pathway.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which is the main photosynthetic pigment in red algae?
questions.explanation: Red algae (Rhodophyta) possess chlorophyll a as their primary pigment and accessory pigments called phycobilins (phycoerythrin and phycocyanin). These absorb blue-green light, allowing red algae to photosynthesize at greater depths in the ocean. They lack chlorophyll b but may have chlorophyll d in some species.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: The pericarp layers in a typical drupe (e.g., mango) are:

options.a: Fleshy exocarp, stony mesocarp, thin endocarp
options.b: Thin exocarp, fleshy mesocarp, stony endocarp
options.c: Stony exocarp, fleshy endocarp, thin mesocarp

questions.explanation: A drupe is a one-seeded fruit with three distinct pericarp layers. In mango and coconut: the exocarp is a thin outer skin, the mesocarp is fleshy and edible, and the endocarp is hard and stony, enclosing the seed. This stony endocarp protects the seed and aids in dispersal.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which structural adaptation is found in xerophytic plants?
questions.explanation: Xerophytes, adapted to dry environments, show morphological and anatomical modifications to reduce water loss. These include a thick waxy cuticle, sunken stomata, reduced leaf surface (sometimes spines), and specialized photosynthetic pathways like CAM. Aerenchyma occurs in hydrophytes, not xerophytes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Pasteur effect refers to:

options.a: Inhibition of aerobic respiration by light
options.b: Decrease in the rate of glycolysis when oxygen is present
options.c: Increase in photorespiration under high O₂
options.d: Suppression of fermentation by high CO₂

questions.explanation: The Pasteur effect describes the observation that glycolysis (anaerobic fermentation) slows down in the presence of oxygen because aerobic respiration is more efficient in ATP yield. Oxygen allows pyruvate to enter the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, reducing the need for rapid glycolytic flux.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In dicot roots, xylem and phloem are arranged on different radii.
Reason (R): This condition is called radial vascular bundle arrangement.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R explains A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: In dicot roots, the vascular system has radial bundles where xylem and phloem are placed on separate radii alternately. This radial arrangement ensures efficient transport in roots. Therefore, both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: In plant tissue culture, the ability of a single cell to regenerate an entire plant is termed:
questions.explanation: Totipotency is the property of a plant cell to give rise to an entire organism. Under suitable nutrient and hormonal conditions in vitro, even a single cell can regenerate shoots, roots, and ultimately a whole plant. This principle forms the basis of micropropagation and plant tissue culture techniques.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Cytokinins are synthesized predominantly in:

options.b: Root apices and transported via xylem

questions.explanation: Cytokinins such as zeatin are synthesized mainly in the meristematic regions of root apices. From there, they are transported upward through the xylem sap to the shoots. Cytokinins promote cell division, delay leaf senescence, and interact with auxins to regulate organogenesis. While developing fruits can also synthesize cytokinins, the primary site is the root tip.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which one of the following statements is true for tracheids?

options.a: They are living cells with nucleus
options.b: They are dead cells with lignified walls

questions.explanation: Tracheids are elongated cells of the xylem that lack protoplasm at maturity and have thick, lignified secondary walls. They conduct water through bordered pits and provide mechanical support. Unlike vessels, which are mainly found in angiosperms, tracheids occur in all vascular plants, including pteridophytes and gymnosperms, making them a more primitive water-conducting element.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Statement I: Phytochrome is a pigment that perceives red and far-red light.
Statement II: It regulates photoperiodic responses like flowering and seed germination.
questions.explanation: Phytochrome is a photoreceptor protein present in plants that exists in two interconvertible forms: Pr (absorbs red light at 660 nm) and Pfr (absorbs far-red light at 730 nm). The Pr form is converted to Pfr in red light, and Pfr is biologically active, regulating responses such as photoperiodic flowering, seed germination, shade avoidance, and circadian rhythms.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which fungal group characteristically exhibits a prolonged dikaryotic (n + n) phase and forms clamp connections?
questions.explanation: Basidiomycota (club fungi) such as mushrooms and rusts exhibit an extended dikaryotic phase, where two haploid nuclei coexist in each cell before karyogamy. Many basidiomycetes develop clamp connections, which are small hyphal outgrowths that ensure each new cell receives one nucleus from each parent strain, maintaining the dikaryotic condition during cell division.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following organisms is used in commercial production of citric acid?
questions.explanation: *Aspergillus niger* is the industrially used fungus for large-scale citric acid production. It ferments carbohydrates such as molasses or sugarcane juice. The citric acid produced is widely used in food, pharmaceuticals, and as a chelating agent. *Penicillium notatum* produces penicillin, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* ferments alcohol, and *Rhizopus stolonifer* is used in organic acid production but not predominantly citric acid.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: During microsporogenesis, separation of the four microspores from the tetrad is brought about by which tapetal enzyme that digests the callose wall?
questions.explanation: After meiosis in microsporocytes, the four haploid microspores are temporarily surrounded by a callose wall. The tapetal cells secrete the enzyme callase, which hydrolyzes callose (a β-1,3-glucan), thereby releasing the individual microspores from the tetrad. This is a crucial step in successful pollen development.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following is used for micropropagation of banana?
questions.explanation: Bananas are sterile and propagated vegetatively. In tissue culture, shoot tip culture is widely used to rapidly multiply banana plants. The shoot tip contains meristematic tissue capable of totipotency, allowing the regeneration of complete, disease-free plants. This method is commercially significant for large-scale banana cultivation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which anther wall layer develops hygroscopic fibrous thickenings that aid in anther dehiscence?
questions.explanation: The endothecium is the subepidermal layer of the anther wall. It develops characteristic fibrous or helical thickenings made of cellulose and lignin. These thickenings respond to drying conditions, creating mechanical tension. As water evaporates, the differential contraction of tissues causes the anther to split open along lines of weakness, releasing pollen grains. Thus, endothecium plays a crucial role in anther dehiscence.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which pair is correctly matched with its type of inflorescence?
questions.explanation: Sunflower (family Asteraceae) exhibits a capitulum (head) type of inflorescence. Many sessile florets are closely packed on a flat receptacle, surrounded by an involucre of bracts. The outer florets are usually ray florets (sterile or female) and the central ones are disc florets (bisexual). This arrangement mimics a single large flower, aiding in pollination. Raceme, spike, and spadix belong to other plant families.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant growth regulator is commonly used to induce bolting in rosette plants such as beet and cabbage?
questions.explanation: Gibberellins are plant hormones that promote stem elongation and flowering. In rosette plants like beet, cabbage, and lettuce, leaves form a compact arrangement near the ground. Application of gibberellic acid (GA₃) overcomes this rosette habit, inducing rapid stem elongation (bolting) and transition to flowering. Auxins mainly regulate apical dominance, ethylene induces ripening, and ABA promotes dormancy.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about plasmodesmata is correct?

options.a: They are interruptions in the cuticle allowing apoplastic flow
options.b: They are cytoplasmic channels connecting adjacent cells, enabling symplastic transport
options.c: They are thickenings of the endodermis that block solute movement
options.d: They are pits in xylem vessels for water movement only

questions.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic cytoplasmic channels that traverse plant cell walls. They connect the cytoplasm of neighboring cells, allowing the movement of ions, metabolites, hormones, and even signaling molecules. Transport through plasmodesmata occurs via the symplastic pathway, which is distinct from apoplastic transport (through cell walls and intercellular spaces). They are critical for intercellular communication and coordination of developmental processes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Compared to the C₃ pathway, how many additional ATP molecules are required per CO₂ fixed in the C₄ pathway (for the CO₂-concentrating shuttle)?
questions.explanation: In the C₄ pathway, atmospheric CO₂ is initially fixed in mesophyll cells by phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) carboxylase, forming oxaloacetate, which is converted to malate/aspartate and transported to bundle sheath cells. To regenerate PEP from pyruvate, the enzyme pyruvate phosphate dikinase consumes 2 ATP per CO₂ fixed. Thus, the C₄ cycle requires 5 ATP + 2 NADPH per CO₂, compared to 3 ATP + 2 NADPH in the C₃ cycle. The extra ATP cost reduces photorespiration losses under high light and temperature.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: The central metal ion in the porphyrin ring of chlorophyll a is:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll a is a porphyrin-based pigment essential for photosynthesis. At the center of its porphyrin ring lies a magnesium (Mg²⁺) ion, coordinated with nitrogen atoms of the tetrapyrrole structure. This magnesium ion plays a critical role in absorbing light energy, particularly in the red and blue regions of the spectrum, and transferring excitation energy to the reaction centers. Iron is central in heme, manganese in the oxygen-evolving complex, and copper in plastocyanin.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which technique is routinely used to produce haploid plants for rapid homozygosity in breeding programs?
questions.explanation: Anther or microspore culture involves culturing immature pollen grains (microspores) in vitro, where they are induced to develop into haploid embryos. These haploid plants can be treated with colchicine to double their chromosome number, producing completely homozygous doubled haploids (DH). This technique accelerates plant breeding programs by bypassing multiple generations of inbreeding. Somatic hybridization, embryo rescue, and meristem tip culture have other applications but do not specifically produce haploids.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which aquatic plant is called 'Terror of Bengal' due to its rapid vegetative propagation?
questions.explanation: Eichhornia crassipes, commonly called water hyacinth, is a free-floating invasive aquatic weed. It reproduces rapidly by vegetative offsets, forming dense mats that clog waterways, deplete oxygen, reduce biodiversity, and interfere with irrigation and navigation. Due to its uncontrollable spread in Bengal's water bodies, it earned the name 'Terror of Bengal'. Other aquatic plants like Hydrilla and Pistia also spread fast but are less problematic.

questions.subject: Zoology
questions.question: Which maternal–fetal incompatibility can cause hemolytic disease of the newborn?
questions.explanation: Hemolytic disease of the newborn (erythroblastosis fetalis) is caused mainly by Rh incompatibility. When an Rh$^-$ mother carries an Rh$^+$ fetus, small amounts of fetal RBCs can enter maternal circulation. The mother's immune system produces anti-D antibodies (IgG type). In subsequent Rh$^+$ pregnancies, these IgG antibodies cross the placenta and destroy fetal red blood cells, leading to hemolysis, anemia, jaundice, and in severe cases hydrops fetalis. ABO incompatibility is usually less severe because anti-A and anti-B are mostly IgM and do not cross the placenta efficiently.

questions.subject: Zoology
questions.question: Which lung volume or capacity equals tidal volume (TV) plus inspiratory reserve volume (IRV)?
questions.explanation: Tidal volume (TV) is the air exchanged in normal quiet breathing (≈500 mL). Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) is the additional volume that can be forcibly inhaled beyond TV (≈2500–3000 mL).
